A
AI Business Systems Analyst  ·  AI Governance  ·  RegTech

The gap between AI deployment and regulatory scrutiny is where I work.

I translate between the engineers who built the system and the auditors who will examine it with the BRDs, BPMN flows, agent pipeline specifications, and governance artefacts that hold up under both.

SR 11-7 and the EU AI Act are not paperwork. They are requirements specifications with legal teeth — and that is a BSA problem.

Why this role exists

High-risk AI obligations are targeting December 2027. The institutions that pass examination will be the ones who started now with requirements an engineer can build from, documentation a regulator can read, and governance that connects the two. That is not engineering. That is not compliance. That is AI Business Systems Analysis.

What a BSA governance portfolio actually contains
— BRD, FRD, and RTM authored for every project — each obligation traced from regulatory article to functional requirement to acceptance criterion, in a format a regulator can read and an engineer can build from.
— BPMN 2.0 process flows designed for each AI system lifecycle — decision gates, HITL escalation paths, incident response, and model drift triggers mapped to named control owners.
— 14 regulatory frameworks cross-mapped in a single traceability matrix — EU AI Act, SR 11-7, PRA SS1/23, GDPR, FATF, Basel III, MiFID II and more, each tied to a designed control with an evidence location a supervisor can find.
— Agent pipeline specifications written as structured BSA artefacts — trigger logic, HITL gates, dead-letter SLAs, data flow diagrams, and exception handling documented before a single line of code is written.

Anusha S
01 / About

Regulation meets
real delivery.

The full picture, plainly stated.

I am an AI Business Systems Analyst with a focused practice at the intersection of AI governance, model risk management, and regulatory compliance for the banking and financial services sector. My work translates obligations from the FCA, EBA, PRA, and EU AI Act enforcement bodies into functional controls, audit-ready artefacts, and agent pipeline specifications that engineering and compliance teams can operate in the real world.

My client delivery experience includes a multi-national investment bank programme replacing manual regulatory reporting and trade surveillance with an API-first compliance platform, embedding MiFID II, Basel III, and EMIR controls and supporting a business case for £1.2M in annual operational savings. Earlier, I served as Global Single Point of Contact on a post-merger ERP integration spanning India, the US, and Australia, translating four conflicting regulatory regimes into one enforceable target operating model for an organisation of 10,000 employees — the same translation work between conflicting requirements and a single deliverable specification that AI governance now demands at greater stakes.

The applied AI governance portfolio was designed to Tier-1 bank standard. It covers governance specifications for three AI systems across three jurisdictions and 14 regulatory frameworks. The artefacts are linked and shown directly below.

On why this work

“In early 2023 I was simultaneously mapping MiFID II obligations to UAT test cases for a Tier-1 investment bank and sitting in MBA lectures on analytics and risk. At some point in that overlap — regulatory text in one window, model outputs in another — it became obvious that the gap between what regulators were demanding and what engineers were building wasn’t a technology problem. It was a requirements problem. Nobody was writing the specification that connected the two. That’s the work I decided to do.”

CSM Certified Anthropic AI Fluency MBA Analytics, Top 5%
02 / Experience

Client delivery,
on record.

Real engagements, real organisations, real outcomes.

Brickendon Consulting Mar 2023 – Jul 2023
Business Analyst & PMO
Remote · India / UK / Poland

Tier-1 investment bank migrating manual regulatory reporting and trade surveillance to an API-first compliance platform, embedding MiFID II, Basel III, and EMIR controls. Lead BA and PMO for the UK programme, owning the full requirements lifecycle across UK, India, and Poland stakeholders. Permanent role held concurrently with MBA studies; transitioned to full-time study following delivery of the RTM and completion of the pre-go-live validation phase.

  • Elicited and documented requirements across 4 cross-border JAD workshops, delivering a BRD covering 12 MiFID II and Basel III use cases and an FRD with 28 functional rules for trade surveillance and capital adequacy reporting. Achieved zero rework through compliance SME review.
  • Mapped 22 regulatory obligations to 63 UAT test cases, building an end-to-end RTM that achieved 100% audit-ready coverage across all in-scope modules and enabled compliance review sign-off with no requirement gaps identified.
  • Authored OpenAPI 3.1 specifications for 15 regulatory API integration points across 3 sprints, contributing to a 30% reduction in post-UAT defects against the bank’s prior project baseline.
  • Designed 3 Power BI governance dashboards for the steering committee, improving regulatory reporting accuracy by 20% and recovering 8 compliance person-hours per week.
  • Modelled As-Is and To-Be workflows using BPMN 2.0 and UML for trade reporting and capital adequacy processes, contributing to a 70% straight-through processing target. Managed RAID log across three time zones and delivered pre-go-live sign-off 5 days ahead of schedule.
Impact £1.2M projected savings 20% reporting efficiency 100% RTM coverage 30% fewer post-UAT defects Go-live 5 days early
HTC Global Services Feb 2022 – Jan 2023
Junior Business Analyst — Global System Integration
Chennai, India · Global (India / UK / US / Australia)

Appointed Global SPOC for post-acquisition consolidation of HTC India, Ciber USA, and CareTech UK into a unified workforce platform across India, UK, US, and Australia for 10,000+ employees.

  • Established a single enforceable baseline accepted by three merging entities, resolving scope conflicts that were blocking integration progress across four countries.
  • Authored BRDs and FRDs translating four conflicting employment-law regimes (Australian Fair Work Act, UK WTR, US FLSA, and GDPR) into one enforceable target operating model.
  • Redesigned approval workflows using BPMN 2.0 and UML, cutting cycle time from 3 days to under 24 hours.
  • Owned end-to-end RTM, RAID, and RACI across full SDLC; built Power BI dashboards for integration KPIs, risk monitoring, and audit tracking surfaced to executive and board-level visibility via QBR.
  • Executed zero-downtime migration across four continents; accelerated global integration rollout by 20%.
Impact 10,000+ users migrated Zero-downtime go-live 29% stakeholder alignment 40% fewer post-migration issues Rollout accelerated 20%
SCSR (ISO 9001) May 2019 – Dec 2022
Project Program Coordinator
Bhopal, India · Multi-state (MP, UP, Chhattisgarh, Rajasthan)

03 / Selected Work

Three projects,
one coherent thesis.

Policy design, compliance pipeline specification, and vendor due diligence. Each project solves a distinct governance problem. Together they close the loop.

Portfolio note: All Horizon Bank PLC materials are demonstration artefacts designed and specified to showcase AI governance and BSA methodology. Every metric, system name, risk register entry, and regulatory mapping is purpose-built for this portfolio. No client-confidential data is used anywhere.
P1AI Governance Framework

AI Model Risk Register & Governance Framework

Three AI systems. 11M decisions a month. No governance layer, no audit trail, and a regulatory enforcement deadline that could not move.The problem was not technical : it was structural. Horizon Bank had no framework that could connect regulatory obligation to a production system and hold up under examination. I designed and specified the complete governance infrastructure from zero: risk register, lifecycle governance, incident response, DPIA, technical documentation, and a Power BI dashboard specification that gives the board a live view of compliance posture across all three systems.

Scope3 systems, 3 jurisdictions
Risk register17 catalogued risks
BPMN diagrams7 process flows
Frameworks14 regulatory alignments
SystemsCREST · SENTINEL · PRISM
Designed scope11M+ decisions/month (simulated)

PRISM AML Monitoring

  • Board-level Model Risk Appetite Statement and Three Lines of Defence structure designed with explicit accountability mapping across the organisation

  • Risk register with 17 risks designed and mapped to Basel SCO60 four-category failure taxonomy with RAG thresholds for executive reporting
  • Independent validation framework specified per system; no self-validation permitted per PRA SS1/23 Principle 4
  • EU AI Act Article 11 Technical Documentation dossier authored for CREST, covering full Annex III justification and Articles 9 to 15 compliance evidence
  • Power BI governance dashboard specified with 11 KPIs, RAG thresholds, drill-through, and role-based access control — designed for board-level compliance posture reporting
View full case study ↗
P2AML Pipeline

PRISM: Multi-Agent AML & EU AI Act Compliance Suite

Four hours of manual triage per alert. A 38% SAR rework rate. Zero regulatory traceability. Every transaction that cleared the queue without a documented rationale was a future audit finding.I designed and specified a five-agent n8n compliance suite to close that gap, prototyped in an n8n sandbox environment. Mode A handles AML transaction triage with a mandatory MLRO approval gate before any SAR is drafted. Mode B performs EU AI Act gap analysis from questionnaire to full compliance report in under ten minutes. Both modes share an immutable Regulatory Audit Log as single source of truth, governed RAG thresholds, a dead-letter queue with a 4-hour SLA, and deterministic override logic so the probabilistic layer cannot compromise a hard compliance boundary.

Agent 01
Risk Analyst
Mode A: Transaction triage, jurisdiction flagging, FATF Rec.16 Travel Rule checks, risk scoring
Agent 02
Compliance Reviewer
Mode A: 11-framework regulatory alignment, escalation routing, MLRO human review gate enforcement
Agent 03
SAR Writer
Mode A: Structured SAR narrative generation with immutable audit log; locked until MLRO approval received
Agent 04
EU AI Act Classifier
Mode B: Annex III prohibited/high-risk classification, Article 10 data governance assessment, risk category assignment
Agent 05
Gap Analyst
Mode B: Articles 9–15 compliance gap analysis, urgency scoring, deadline recommendations, full audit trail

P3RegTech Intelligence

External Intelligence: RegTech Agent & Vendor Risk Toolkit

Three days to produce a single regulatory briefing note. Four to six weeks for vendor due diligence. The compliance team was reacting to regulation instead of anticipating it.I designed and specified two n8n agent pipelines to close that intelligence gap. Mode A monitors FCA, EBA, and RBI feeds daily, maps every publication's impact directly to CREST, SENTINEL, and PRISM, applies heuristic confidence scoring, and enforces mandatory human review for anything flagged HIGH or CRITICAL. Mode B applies a deterministic EU AI Act Art.28 scoring rubric, generates a due diligence report via GPT-4o, and auto-creates risk register entries in Project 1 post-MLRO approval , closing the governance loop without manual handoff.

ArchitectureTwo-mode n8n pipeline
RegulatorsFCA · EBA · RBI
Vendor rubric4-domain Art.28 scoring
HITL gateKeyword + severity triggers
Audit trail100% auditable Notion log (design spec)
IntegrationsNotion · Slack · Project 1
Regulatory briefing3 days↓<2 hrsPublication to
briefing (projected)
Vendor assessment4-6 wks↓<24 hrsQuestionnaire to
DDR (projected)
Manual effort18 hrs↓<2 hrsPer week, exceptions
only (projected)
P3 Artefacts: n8n pipeline specifications · Click to enlarge
↗
Mode A: RegTech Intelligence
Scheduled ingest · Regulatory Feed · Impact Assessor · Briefing Generator
↗
Mode B: Vendor Risk Assessor
Questionnaire ingest · EU AI Act Art.28 scoring · Due Diligence Report
  • Three-layer governance design separating intelligence (probabilistic GPT-4o), control (deterministic HITL triggers and scoring), and monitoring (heuristic confidence, post-review sampling, audit log)
  • HITL specified through rule-based triggers: impact = HIGH/CRITICAL, or enforcement keywords (fine, penalty, cease and desist, immediate effect), or vendor risk score ≥61
  • Deterministic vendor scoring rubric designed: EU AI Act Art.28 documentation (30 pts), Model Risk (25 pts), Data Protection (25 pts), Incident Response SLA (20 pts), with Art.28 override forcing CRITICAL if no documentation
  • Post-review sampling protocol specified: 20% of MEDIUM and 10% of LOW items randomly audited within 5-10 days to catch probabilistic-layer drift
  • End-to-end integration design with Project 1 Model Risk Register: Mode A specified to link briefings to existing risks, Mode B designed to auto-create new risk register entries after MLRO approval
  • Full BRD, FRD, and PRD delivered with MoSCoW prioritisation, user stories, Gherkin acceptance criteria, release criteria, and audit challenge scenarios documented

AI Regulation & Governance

07.5 / Live

Currently
tracking.

Regulatory developments I am actively following and can discuss in depth.

PRA · SS1/23 Follow-up

How Tier-1 firms are operationalising the PRA’s model risk management expectations — specifically, where boards are pushing back on independent validation requirements and how Chief Model Risk Officers are structuring the three-lines response. Directly relevant to the SENTINEL and CREST governance work in my portfolio.

PRA SS1/23  ·  Model Risk  ·  Validation Governance
EU AI Act · Article 9

Implementation guidance on Article 9 risk management system obligations for high-risk AI — particularly how the European AI Office is interpreting “continuous iterative process” for credit-scoring and AML systems listed under Annex III. The PRISM and CREST systems in my portfolio are directly in scope and I have mapped their controls to these obligations.
